items.original: «Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон»
items.translation: “Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.”
items.reference: “Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The mock-heroic phrasing (“Vague doubts haunt me…”) is preserved, and the clipped parallelism (“Shpak’s… the ambassador’s…”) keeps the comedic detective-like inference. No further evaluation needed per instructions.

items.original: «Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.»
items.translation: “Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.”
items.reference: “Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The repetitive, banal politeness and self-introduction as “the Tsar” (the core comic mechanism) is fully preserved.
Identical to the approved/reference translation. The authoritarian performative demand is preserved cleanly and punchily.

items.translation: “Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.”
items.reference: “Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The archaic/high-register melancholy is maintained, supporting the era-clash humor.

items.translation: “They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!”
items.reference: “They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The subject assignment (“the demons”) and the panicked repetition are preserved, keeping the joke of technological misunderstanding.

items.translation: “Behold the power of the life-giving cross!”
items.reference: “Behold the power of the life-giving cross!”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The elevated religious diction is preserved, sustaining the comic contrast with the Soviet/technical setting.
Identical to the approved/reference translation. The idiomatic rendering keeps the emcee-like command energy without awkward literalism (“Everyone dances!”).
Identical to the approved/reference translation. The breezy self-satisfaction and criminal opportunism are conveyed in a natural English idiom.

items.original: «Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!»
items.translation: “Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!”
items.reference: “Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. Crucially avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar,” preserving the intended Soviet-status inversion joke and preventing confusion.

items.original: «Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три»
items.translation: “Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.”
items.reference: “Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. Preserves the comic inventory rhythm, the self-important “domestic” qualifier, and the trailing “Three” that signals the escalating/exaggerated tally.

items.original: «И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…»
items.translation: “And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...”
items.reference: “And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...”
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The calm, sing-song fatalism is preserved, keeping the darkly comic normalization of psychiatric intervention.
